title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A national heritage organisation has commissioned a report on the risks facing cultural heritage sites in your country as a result of climate change and overtourism. Your report should identify the main threats, assess their impact on specific types of heritage sites, and recommend a coordinated strategy to improve resilience and manage visitor pressure more effectively.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Climate Change and Overtourism Risks to National Heritage Sites**

**Introduction**
This report identifies the principal risks posed by climate change and overtourism to cultural heritage sites across the country, assesses their differential impact by site type, and recommends a coordinated national strategy to improve resilience and manage visitor pressure. Evidence draws on assessments by conservation bodies, visitor management data from fifteen major sites, and a review of international best practice.

**Principal Threats**
Climate change poses four categories of risk to heritage sites: accelerated stone and mortar deterioration from temperature fluctuations and freeze-thaw cycles; increased structural damage from extreme weather events including flooding and storm surges; coastal erosion affecting maritime heritage; and the emergence of new biological threats — including invasive plant species and humidity-tolerant fungi — damaging interior environments in historic buildings. Overtourism independently contributes through footfall-related erosion, vibration from visitor transport, atmospheric pollution and the physical wear caused by concentrated access to fragile surfaces.

**Impact by Site Type**
Coastal and riverine sites face the highest combined risk exposure. Urban historic centres experience the most acute overtourism pressures, particularly during peak summer months.

**Recommendations**
Three strategic measures are recommended: mandatory climate vulnerability assessments for all Grade I listed sites within three years; a national visitor dispersal programme redistributing tourism pressure to under-visited secondary sites; and a cross-departmental fund combining heritage and environmental resilience budgets.

**Conclusion**
The interdependency of climate and tourism risks requires joined-up policy responses that treat heritage resilience as inseparable from broader environmental and economic planning.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You are a member of a local historical society. You have learned that a property developer has submitted a planning application to demolish a nineteenth-century building of significant historical and architectural interest in your town in order to build a commercial retail development. Write a formal letter to the local planning authority objecting to the application, explaining the historical and architectural significance of the building, arguing for its preservation and potential adaptive reuse, and requesting that it be granted listed building status.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Planning Officer,

I write on behalf of the Thornfield Historical Society to register our formal objection to planning application reference 2024/PA/0872, submitted by Meridian Developments Ltd for the demolition of the Whitmore Goods Warehouse at 14–22 Canal Street, and to request that the planning authority defer any decision pending an assessment of the building's suitability for listed status.

The Whitmore Goods Warehouse is one of fewer than a dozen intact examples of Victorian industrial canal-side architecture remaining in the region. Constructed in 1881 by the engineer Thomas Fairweather, the building retains its original cast-iron loading crane, cobbled loading yard, and distinctive polychrome brickwork facade — features explicitly identified as noteworthy in the 1994 Buildings at Risk survey conducted by the county council's heritage team. It represents a tangible material link to the commercial and industrial history that shaped this town's development and identity.

We do not oppose development on this site; we oppose the permanent destruction of an irreplaceable historic asset when alternative approaches exist. Adaptive reuse schemes — converting industrial heritage buildings into mixed commercial, residential or cultural uses — have proven economically successful and architecturally enriching in comparable projects in Leeds, Manchester and Bristol. We would strongly urge the authority to require the applicant to demonstrate that adaptive reuse options have been genuinely and thoroughly explored before any demolition consent is considered.

We would welcome the opportunity to present further evidence at a planning hearing.

Yours faithfully,
Professor Eleanor Marsh
Chair, Thornfield Historical Society

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a book called 'Whose Heritage? Museums, Colonialism and the Battle for Cultural Memory' which examines the history of how major Western museums acquired their collections, the ethical arguments for and against repatriation of cultural artefacts, and the possible models for international cultural partnerships that could benefit both holding institutions and countries of origin. Write a review for a cultural studies journal evaluating the depth of the book's historical analysis, the balance of its treatment of competing arguments, and the originality of its proposed solutions.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Essential, Probing and Genuinely Fair: A Review of 'Whose Heritage?'**

The debate over the repatriation of cultural artefacts held in Western museum collections has intensified considerably in recent years, yet the published literature has tended to divide along predictable lines — institutional defences of universal museum principles on one side, and decolonisation frameworks demanding unconditional return on the other. Dr Nadia Osei-Asante's 'Whose Heritage?' is a welcome and largely successful attempt to move beyond this polarisation.

The book opens with a meticulous historical account of acquisition practices at six major European institutions, drawing on archival material to establish, with scholarly precision, the range of circumstances — from outright seizure during military campaigns to more ambiguous transactions conducted under colonial power asymmetries — through which collections were assembled. This section is the book's most original contribution, and it significantly enriches a debate that too often proceeds on generalisation rather than historical specificity.

Equally impressive is Osei-Asante's treatment of the counterarguments. The concerns of holding institutions — regarding fragmentation of contextually linked collections, conservation capacity in origin countries and the legal complexity of title — are engaged with seriously rather than dismissed. This intellectual generosity considerably strengthens the book's credibility.

The proposed framework of 'dynamic custodianship' — encompassing long-term loans, collaborative curation, shared digital access and phased physical returns — is thoughtful and grounded in existing precedents, though the author acknowledges that political will remains the binding constraint.

This is an essential, measured and intellectually serious contribution to one of the most important cultural debates of our time.
